synopsis: In an isolated 18th-century rural European village, undertaker Marek Volek is the one man trusted to tend the dead—and the first man blamed when graves begin to appear torn open in the night. As fear spreads through the churchyard and the villagers whisper of theft, sacrilege, and darker appetites, Marek uncovers signs that point not to a living criminal but to a ghoul born from old famine, neglect, and an unquiet grave. With only a skeptical constable, a grief-stricken widow, a cautious priest, and an uncanny herbalist around him, Marek must hunt the creature while the village closes in. The story is a gothic folk-horror mystery about duty, reputation, and whether saving others matters more than being believed.

character_data.name: Marek Volek
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: waist-up portrait of a rural 18th-century European undertaker, lean man in his early 30s, pale and tired eyes, dark wool coat and high-collared linen shirt, mud on cuffs, solemn expression, candlelit shadows, realistic painterly visual novel portrait
dialogue_examples: The dead are simpler than the living; they only ask to be left in peace.
If I had robbed these graves, I would not have left them in such a state.
Mud, teeth, and hunger opened that coffin, not any spade of mine.
Believe me or bind me, Constable, but while you hesitate, something feeds.
I can endure being hated. I cannot endure letting this continue.

character_data.name: Father Benedek
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: waist-up portrait of an elderly village priest in 18th-century rural Europe, stern lined face, thinning gray hair, black cassock with worn wool cloak, small wooden cross in hand, guarded compassionate eyes, muted church candlelight, realistic painterly visual novel portrait
dialogue_examples: Superstition is tinder; speak carefully before the whole village burns.
You serve the dead, Marek, so help me keep faith among the living.
A desecrated grave is not just a crime; it is a wound to every soul here.
If evil walks, it will not be driven out by panic.

character_data.name: Marta Dolek
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: waist-up portrait of a grieving village widow in 18th-century rural Europe, weathered face, red-rimmed eyes, dark headscarf and plain homespun dress, clenched jaw, cold morning light, realistic painterly visual novel portrait
dialogue_examples: My husband was buried with his wedding ring, and now the coffin lies open like a butchered pig.
Who else keeps the keys to the graves but the undertaker?
Do not ask me for patience when the earth itself spits our dead back out.
If the law will not act, grieving hands will.

character_data.name: Constable Ivo Klein
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: waist-up portrait of a rural constable in 18th-century Europe, broad-shouldered man in worn blue coat, leather belt, tricorn hat tucked under one arm, practical guarded expression, stubble, overcast outdoor lighting, realistic painterly visual novel portrait
dialogue_examples: I deal in facts, not midnight tales, but facts are in short supply this week.
Every track in that cemetery leads back to your gate, Marek.
Give me proof I can show a crowd, or I will have to give them a suspect instead.
I would rather chain a monster than a neighbor; help me know the difference.

character_data.name: Eliska Varga
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: waist-up portrait of a village herbalist in 18th-century rural Europe, sharp dark eyes, braided hair under a shawl, satchel of herbs and bone charms, practical layered skirts, intelligent wary expression, moonlit forest tones, realistic painterly visual novel portrait
dialogue_examples: Some hungers survive the body; old women know this even when priests do not.
Look at the bite, not the wound. This was feeding, not theft.
A ghoul is still a creature of habit—earth, carrion, and the path of least prayer.
If the village wants reason, we may have to catch its nightmare in daylight.

character_data.name: Janek Rusek, the Ghoul
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: waist-up portrait of a feral ghoul in 18th-century rural Europe, emaciated corpse-pale man with remnants of a sexton's coat, muddy skin, blackened nails, sunken eyes, broken teeth, damp grave soil clinging to him, horrific but tragic, moonlit realistic painterly visual novel portrait
dialogue_examples: You seal the earth and call it kindness. I remember the hunger beneath.
I kept ringing the bell, and none of you came.
The dead do not mind me. It is the living who cling.
Starving men become beasts long before the grave takes them.

location_data.name: Saint Bartholomew Churchyard
location_data.location_image_prompt: 18th-century rural European cemetery at dawn, crooked stone graves, fresh turned earth, damp grass, small hilltop church, iron gate, low mist, crows, subdued gothic folk-horror atmosphere

location_data.name: Undertaker's Cottage and Mortuary
location_data.location_image_prompt: humble timber-and-stone undertaker's cottage interior and mortuary workroom, coffin boards, funeral linens, iron tools, candles, drying herbs, rain-streaked window, 18th-century rural Europe, somber warm shadows

location_data.name: Village Square
location_data.location_image_prompt: muddy 18th-century rural European village square, timber houses, market cart, well, church bell tower in distance, anxious villagers gathered at dusk, overcast sky, grounded folk-horror mood

location_data.name: Ruined Marsh Chapel
location_data.location_image_prompt: collapsed stone chapel in a marsh, broken stained glass, reeds, stagnant black water, exposed crypt steps, moonlight and fog, decayed 18th-century rural Europe, eerie folk-horror composition

scene_data.narrtive_summary: During a dawn burial at Saint Bartholomew Churchyard, Marek discovers a nearby grave torn open from below and its shroud dragged into the mud. Father Benedek tries to calm the mourners, Marta loudly accuses Marek of profiting from the dead, and Constable Ivo begins a formal inquiry on the spot. The prologue establishes Marek's lonely role in the village, the first wave of suspicion against him, and the unsettling evidence that the desecration may not be the work of an ordinary thief.

In the village square, rumor turns public as Ivo questions Marek before an anxious crowd. Marta pushes the simplest explanation—that the undertaker has used his access to rob graves—while Father Benedek urges patience before the village descends into mob justice. By inspecting the pattern of violated burials and the missing grave goods, Marek realizes the desecrations follow hunger and freshness rather than profit, sharpening his sense that something inhuman is choosing the dead.

Back in the undertaker's cottage and mortuary, Marek secretly asks Eliska Varga to examine the soil, scraps of cloth, and bite marks left behind. Eliska identifies signs from village folklore and old famine tales that point to a ghoul rather than a grave robber, and she connects the pattern to an abandoned chapel near the marsh where plague dead and paupers were once buried. The act ends with Marek deciding to set a night watch in the cemetery, knowing that if he is seen prowling among graves after dark, suspicion will only deepen.

At night in the churchyard, Marek and Eliska keep watch beside a freshly dug grave used as bait. Janek the ghoul emerges from the darkness—fast, starving, and still half recognizably human—and Marek wounds it in a desperate struggle, recovering a rotted piece of sexton's clothing as it flees. The victory turns sour when villagers, drawn by the commotion, find Marek alone over another opened grave with blood on his hands and no monster in sight.

By morning the village square has turned hostile, with Marta demanding punishment and others insisting the undertaker be jailed before another grave is touched. Ivo, torn between duty and doubt, gives Marek only until dawn of the next day to produce undeniable proof before arresting him, while Father Benedek struggles to prevent violence. Eliska identifies the cloth scrap as belonging to Janek Rusek, a sexton believed dead during the famine years, and directs Marek toward the ruined marsh chapel where the truth may still be buried.

Marek and Eliska descend beneath the ruined marsh chapel and discover Janek's lair among collapsed crypts, old parish records, stolen grave goods, and the gnawed remains of burial shrouds. Janek reveals himself as a former sexton abandoned during famine and transformed by starvation into a creature that now feeds on the dead, making the horror both supernatural and tragically human. Marek gains evidence that can expose the truth, but Janek breaks free and races back toward the village graves, forcing Marek to decide whether his next move is about proving what he found or stopping what he unleashed.

The climax returns to Saint Bartholomew Churchyard as Janek attacks among the graves while villagers, drawn by bells and shouting, arrive in time to witness only fragments of the chaos. Marek can destroy the ghoul at once by triggering a prepared fire and collapse on consecrated ground, a choice that saves lives but ruins much of the physical evidence that could clear his name; or he can risk holding Janek long enough for Ivo, Father Benedek, and the villagers to see the truth plainly, potentially restoring his innocence at the cost of further harm and desecration. This scene resolves the story's central moral question: whether Marek values duty to the vulnerable dead and living above his own vindication, or whether being believed is necessary to do that duty at all.

At dawn in the village square, the community reckons with what happened in the churchyard. If Marek chose to stop the ghoul first, the village is safer but many still fear him, leaving him vindicated only in the eyes of a few loyal witnesses; if he chose to secure public proof first, his innocence is acknowledged more fully, but the cost of hesitation is mourned in fresh grief. The denouement closes on Marek's place in the village and the bitter truth that the one who keeps faith with the dead may never be fully trusted by the living.
